The likely issue is that some helpers are only connected through higher-level paths that deadcode isn’t retaining, so I’m tracing the exact call sites and then I’ll decide whether to keep them via production plumbing or replace them with a simpler primitive.

1	package agent
     2	
     3	// prompt_file_read.go centralizes the bounded read for user-supplied
     4	// --prompt files (FEAT-022 §8). All agent-side prompt-file readers
     5	// route through readPromptFileBounded so that oversize inputs hard-fail
     6	// with an actionable error rather than silently truncating or being
     7	// shipped to a provider as-is.
     8	
     9	import (
    10		"errors"
    11		"fmt"
    12	
    13		"github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/evidence"
    14	)
    15	
    16	// promptFileCapBytes is the byte cap applied to user-supplied --prompt
    17	// files. It exists as a package-level variable (rather than a literal)
    18	// so tests can lower the cap to keep oversize fixtures small. Production
    19	// callers must not mutate it.
    20	var promptFileCapBytes = evidence.DefaultMaxPromptBytes
    21	
    22	// readPromptFileBounded reads path as a user-supplied prompt file,
    23	// hard-failing when the file exceeds the configured prompt cap.
    24	// On oversize the returned error names the source path, observed size,
    25	// configured cap, and points the operator at the config key that
    26	// adjusts the cap (.ddx/config.yaml:evidence_caps.max_prompt_bytes).
    27	func readPromptFileBounded(path string) ([]byte, error) {
    28		cap := promptFileCapBytes
    29		content, err := evidence.ReadFileHardFail(path, cap, path)
    30		if err != nil {
    31			if errors.Is(err, evidence.ErrOversize) {
    32				return nil, fmt.Errorf(
    33					"prompt file %q exceeds cap: %v (configurable via .ddx/config.yaml:evidence_caps.max_prompt_bytes)",
    34					path, err)
    35			}
    36			return nil, err
    37		}
    38		return content, nil
    39	}

1	package evidence
     2	
     3	import (
     4		"strings"
     5	)
     6	
     7	// InlineAssembly is the result of AssembleInline.
     8	type InlineAssembly struct {
     9		Prompt   string
    10		Sections []EvidenceAssemblySection
    11		// Overflow is true when, after all trimming, the assembled prompt
    12		// would still exceed total. Callers (reviewer/grader) MUST NOT
    13		// dispatch when Overflow is true (FEAT-022 §7) and should emit the
    14		// appropriate context_overflow outcome class instead.
    15		Overflow bool
    16	}
    17	
    18	// AssembleInline assembles a prompt by joining priority-ordered
    19	// sections under a total byte budget (FEAT-022 §2 inline-with-cap
    20	// strategy). Per-section caps and floor semantics are honored via the
    21	// SectionInput fields. Sections are joined verbatim with a single
    22	// newline separator between non-empty bodies.
    23	func AssembleInline(sections []SectionInput, total int) InlineAssembly {
    24		fit := FitSections(sections, total)
    25	
    26		var b strings.Builder
    27		for i, s := range fit.Included {
    28			if i > 0 {
    29				b.WriteByte('\n')
    30			}
    31			b.WriteString(s.Content)
    32		}
    33		out := b.String()
    34	
    35		overflow := len(out) > total
    36		return InlineAssembly{
    37			Prompt:   out,
    38			Sections: fit.Sections,
    39			Overflow: overflow,
    40		}
